figures.gloss: The Leicestershire weaver's son (1624–1691) who, after years wandering among England's Civil War sects, heard 'there is one, even Christ Jesus, that can speak to thy condition' (1647) and preached that Christ has come to teach his people himself. His 1652 vision on Pendle Hill and sermon to a thousand Seekers at Firbank Fell launched the movement; the Valiant Sixty, itinerant preachers from the Westmorland north, carried it across Britain within two years.
figures.facet: A founder who abolished the founder's office: Fox claimed no monopoly on the Light he preached, so the movement he organized — into a discipline of meetings rather than a church under a man — could survive him without a succession crisis.
figures.provenance: The Journal of George Fox (pub. 1694, ed. Thomas Ellwood); William C. Braithwaite, The Beginnings of Quakerism (1912); H. Larry Ingle, First Among Friends (1994).

figures.gloss: The core Quaker conviction that 'that of God in every one' — the Light of Christ within — is directly available to each person without priest, sacrament, or ordained mediation. Fox charged Friends to 'walk cheerfully over the world, answering that of God in every one'; Robert Barclay's Apology gave the doctrine its systematic theology.
figures.facet: It relocates revelation from the past tense to the present: Scripture remains authoritative for Friends, but as the record of the same Spirit that still speaks — the boldest immediatist claim to survive within the Protestant family.
figures.provenance: The Journal of George Fox (1694); Robert Barclay, An Apology for the True Christian Divinity (Latin 1676, English 1678).

figures.gloss: Worship as expectant waiting: Friends sit in silence with no liturgy, sermon, hymn, or clergy, until anyone — of any age or sex — feels moved by the Spirit to rise and speak. The name 'Quaker' began as mockery of the trembling that seized early Friends in these gatherings.
figures.facet: The most radical worship-form in Christianity's western family: it deletes every outward instrument of the cult — building sanctity, sacrament, priesthood, text-in-use — and stakes everything on the gathered stillness itself.
figures.provenance: The Journal of George Fox (1694); Barclay, Apology, Proposition XI (1676); Pink Dandelion, An Introduction to Quakerism (2007).

figures.gloss: Not a creed but a discipline of life: the refusal of all war ('we utterly deny all outward wars and strife,' declared to Charles II in 1660/61), of oaths, of hat-honour and flattering titles, of luxury in dress and speech. Friends addressed judges and kings as 'thee' and went to prison by the thousand rather than swear or fight.
figures.facet: Ethics as evidence: the testimonies treat conduct itself as the argument for the faith — a life 'in the virtue of that life and power that takes away the occasion of all wars' is the claim, and the believer's body is where it is published.
figures.provenance: A Declaration from the Harmless and Innocent People of God, called Quakers, to Charles II (1660/61); Fox's Journal; Hugh Barbour & J. William Frost, The Quakers (1988).

figures.gloss: The mistress of Swarthmoor Hall (1614–1702), convinced by Fox in 1652, whose home became the movement's nerve-center and funding base — she ran its correspondence network and relief fund for imprisoned Friends. Her Women's Speaking Justified (1666), written from Lancaster prison, is a founding text of the Quaker claim that the Spirit authorizes women's ministry; she married Fox in 1669.
figures.facet: She shows that early Quakerism's equality testimony was structural, not rhetorical: women preached, traveled in ministry, and from 1671 held their own decision-making meetings — a formal share of church government almost unique in the 17th century.
figures.provenance: Margaret Fell, Women's Speaking Justified (1666); her Swarthmoor correspondence; Bonnelyn Young Kunze, Margaret Fell and the Rise of Quakerism (1994).

figures.gloss: The New Jersey tailor (1720–1772) whose quiet, decades-long ministry against slaveholding — visiting slave-owning Friends one household at a time, refusing to write bills of sale, wearing undyed clothes to boycott slave-produced dye — moved Philadelphia Yearly Meeting to bar slaveholding in 1776. His posthumous Journal (1774) became the classic of Quaker spirituality.
figures.facet: The abolition witness in person: Woolman worked by convincement rather than denunciation, and his method — the patient, face-to-face moral audit — made Friends the first religious body in the Atlantic world to disown slaveholding, a century before emancipation.
figures.provenance: The Journal of John Woolman (1774); Some Considerations on the Keeping of Negroes (1754); Germantown Quaker Protest (1688); Thomas P. Slaughter, The Beautiful Soul of John Woolman (2008).

figures.gloss: The London-born convinced Friend (1644–1718) whose 1670 trial with William Mead — the jury's acquittal upheld despite the judge's demand for a guilty verdict — helped establish English juries' right to reach an independent verdict (Bushel's Case). Given proprietary rights to American land in settlement of a royal debt, he founded Pennsylvania in 1681 as a 'holy experiment': a colony with no established church, broad religious liberty, and — unusually for the era — negotiated treaties of purchase and friendship with the Lenape rather than simple displacement.
figures.facet: He tests the testimonies at the scale of a government: Pennsylvania asks whether a state can actually run on refusal-of-oaths, religious liberty, and treaty-good-faith rather than merely a meetinghouse's internal discipline — an experiment whose success historians still debate, given Pennsylvania's departures from Penn's Indian policy after his death.
figures.provenance: William Penn, The Frame of Government of Pennsylvania (1682); the 1670 Penn-Mead trial record; Andrew Murphy, William Penn: A Life (2018); Mary Maples Dunn, William Penn: Politics and Conscience (1967).

figures.gloss: A Yorkshire former parliamentary soldier and Fox's early co-equal in ministry (1618–1660), whose growing following venerated him in near-messianic terms; in October 1656 he rode into Bristol accompanied by supporters singing 'Holy, Holy, Holy' and strewing garments before him in imitation of Christ's entry into Jerusalem. Parliament, not a church court, tried him for blasphemy and imposed a savage sentence — pillory, whipping through two cities, his tongue bored with a hot iron, his forehead branded 'B' for blasphemer. He died within three years of release; his last words ('There is a spirit which I feel...') are still read at Quaker meetings.
figures.facet: The crisis he triggered is the movement's own hinge: afterward, Fox and the emerging leadership moved decisively to organize Quakerism into disciplined, corporate meetings capable of restraining individual charisma — the mechanism that let the sect survive as an institution rather than dissolve into competing prophets.
figures.provenance: Parliamentary trial record (1656, Journals of the House of Commons); The Journal of George Fox (1694; his account of the breach with Nayler); Leo Damrosch, The Sorrows of the Quaker Jesus: James Nayler and the Puritan Crackdown on the Free Spirit (1996).

figures.gloss: The Aberdeen-born Scottish Friend (1648–1690), educated in Calvinist and Catholic theology before his convincement, whose Latin An Apology for the True Christian Divinity (1676, English 1678) is the movement's one work of systematic theology — fifteen propositions defending the Inner Light, silent worship, and the rejection of outward sacraments in the scholastic idiom his university-trained critics could not dismiss as mere enthusiasm.
figures.facet: He translates Quakerism's experiential claims into the era's academic argumentative register — proof the movement could survive contact with formal theology, not persecution alone, and the reason his Apology (rather than Fox's Journal) became the text through which Continental Europe first encountered Quaker doctrine.
figures.provenance: Robert Barclay, An Apology for the True Christian Divinity (Latin 1676, English 1678); D. Elton Trueblood, Robert Barclay (1968).

figures.gloss: The 1827–28 division of Philadelphia Yearly Meeting, and subsequently other American yearly meetings, between followers of the itinerant minister Elias Hicks, who stressed the Inner Light's priority over scripture and creed, and the 'Orthodox' party, who emphasized biblical authority and evangelical Protestant doctrine closer to the era's wider revivalist current. The split produced parallel, mutually non-recognizing yearly meetings across the United States for over a century.
figures.facet: It is the fault line still running under Quakerism's split character today — unprogrammed, Hicksite-descended meetings reading as liberal Protestant/universalist next to programmed, pastor-led evangelical Friends churches — a roster that presents Quakerism as one thing understates a two-century-old bifurcation.
figures.provenance: H. Larry Ingle, Quakers in Conflict: The Hicksite Reformation (1986); Thomas D. Hamm, The Transformation of American Quakerism, 1800-1907 (1988).

figures.gloss: Quaker corporate decision-making conducted as an extension of worship: Friends gather for 'meeting for business,' a clerk states the matter, and members speak as moved until the clerk discerns unity — the 'sense of the meeting' — and minutes it. There is no vote and no majority rule; a small minority holding a conscientious objection can hold a decision, forcing further waiting ('seasoning') rather than a forced resolution.
figures.facet: It extends silent worship's central claim — that truth emerges through gathered waiting rather than argument — into the mundane business of running a religious body, making Quaker governance procedurally inseparable from Quaker theology.
figures.provenance: Michael J. Sheeran, Beyond Majority Rule: Voteless Decisions in the Religious Society of Friends (1983); Britain Yearly Meeting, Quaker Faith and Practice (Church Government chapters).

figures.gloss: The Norwich-born banker and minister (1788–1847), the most influential 19th-century evangelical Quaker, who argued Scripture — not the Inner Light alone — was the primary rule of faith and promoted pastoral leadership and programmed elements in worship against the older Quietist unprogrammed tradition. His American tours (1837–40) precipitated a split between his 'Gurneyite' followers and the conservative 'Wilburites,' a rupture distinct from, but entangled with, the Hicksite-Orthodox division.
figures.facet: He is the root of the branch that is today's numerical majority: the pastoral, programmed-worship, evangelical Quakerism carried by 19th-century missions — including, from 1902, the Friends Africa Industrial Mission to Kenya, which grew into East Africa Yearly Meeting, now the world's largest Quaker body. This roster's 17th–18th-century, unprogrammed-flavored figures represent a style that, however dominant in founding history, is a minority by current numbers.
figures.provenance: Joseph John Gurney, Observations on the Religious Peculiarities of the Society of Friends (1824); David E. Swift, Joseph John Gurney: Banker, Reformer and Quaker (1962); Thomas D. Hamm, The Transformation of American Quakerism (1988); Friends Africa Industrial Mission records (Kenya, est. 1902).

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: direct descent — Friends emerged from the radical Puritan and Seeker wing of the English Reformation, driving the universal priesthood to its terminus: no clergy, no outward sacraments, the Light immediate in every person.
Within the same family, the formal principle inverted: Friends set the living Spirit above the letter — Scripture is confirmed by the Light that gave it, and worship needs no book at all.
Fox's 1647 'opening' — one, even Christ Jesus, who can speak to thy condition — is the experiential seed the Inner Light doctrine formalizes.
Woolman's abolition ministry is the equality testimony enacted: the same Light in every person makes owning a person an offense against God's presence in them.

GOLD SEAM (origin): the Wardleys' Manchester society (c.1747) were 'Shaking Quakers' — ex-Friends whose silent waiting broke into Camisard-style ecstatic prophecy; Ann Lee's Believers carried both the name and the waiting-then-shaking worship to America.

Cross-tradition seam, held low: both describe a directly perceptible inner or uncreated light attained through disciplined stillness and prayer — a suggestive structural rhyme between hesychast theosis and the Quaker Inner Light, with no historical contact between the two.

Both reject an intermediary clergy and a state church as part of the Reformation's radical/spiritualist wing, though Quakers arise in England over a century later with no direct Anabaptist contact claimed.
Intra-tradition: Pennsylvania is the peace and equality testimonies enacted at governmental scale, mirroring the Woolman-testimonies pairing already in this roster.
Intra-tradition: Fox's early equal in ministry whose 1656 discipline crisis reshaped the movement Fox went on to organize.
Intra-tradition: the Apology is the direct systematic-theology source already cited in this roster's own Inner Light entry.
Intra-tradition: the Hicksite position takes the Inner Light doctrine to its liberal extreme; the Orthodox position reasserts scriptural authority against it.
Intra-tradition: the same silence-and-discernment theology extended from worship into the conduct of church business.

Intra-tradition: a related but distinct 19th-century American fracture — the Gurneyite/Wilburite split ran within the Orthodox branch itself, over pastoral leadership and programmed worship, not scripture-vs-Light.

Same person, opposite economy of access: early Friends held the Inner Light to BE Christ — 'the true Light which lighteth every man' (John 1:9) — present immediately and universally, where the source tradition locates Christ decisively in the incarnate, risen person mediated by church and sacrament.

dossier.essence: The Religious Society of Friends is the Christian body that emerged from the radical Puritan and Seeker ferment of the English Civil War, launched by George Fox's preaching from 1647 and the mass convincements at Firbank Fell in 1652. Its central claim is that the Light of Christ is directly present in every person — 'that of God in every one' — without priest, sacrament, or outward mediation. Roughly 400,000 Friends worldwide now divide between the unprogrammed silent tradition of Britain and the American east and the programmed, pastor-led evangelical majority, whose numerical center is Kenya.
dossier.cosmos: Quaker cosmology is inherited Protestant Christianity made doctrinally quiet: creation, providence, and the person of Christ are affirmed, but the tradition's distinctive move is temporal, not structural — revelation is continuing, and the Spirit that gave Scripture still speaks in the gathered meeting. Early Friends preached a realized eschatology ('Christ has come to teach his people himself'); evangelical Friends today hold ordinary Protestant expectations of resurrection and judgment, while the liberal unprogrammed branch is creedless and deliberately reticent about what follows death.
dossier.practice: Unprogrammed meetings worship in expectant silence, with no clergy, liturgy, or outward sacrament, until anyone present feels moved to speak; programmed meetings, the world majority, worship with pastors, sermons, and hymns. Decisions are made in meetings for business by discerning the 'sense of the meeting' rather than by vote, and the testimonies — peace, simplicity, integrity, equality — function as a discipline of life: historic Friends refused oaths, war, and hat-honour, and went to prison by the thousand for it. Women have preached and held their own meetings for church government since the 17th century.
dossier.canon: The Bible remains authoritative, read as the record of a Spirit still speaking; alongside it stand Fox's Journal (1694), Robert Barclay's Apology (1678) as the systematic theology, and each yearly meeting's evolving book of Faith and Practice.
dossier.reflects: The immediatist terminus of the Reformation — what a tradition looks like when every mediating instrument of the cult (priest, sacrament, liturgy, even the sermon) is deleted and the whole weight rests on unmediated presence and the conduct it produces.
sources: George Fox, 'The Journal of George Fox' (1694; ed. Norman Penney, 1911)
Robert Barclay, 'An Apology for the True Christian Divinity' (1678)
William C. Braithwaite, 'The Beginnings of Quakerism' (1912)

Robert Barclay, 'An Apology for the True Christian Divinity' (1678)
William C. Braithwaite, 'The Beginnings of Quakerism' (1912)
